claims.normalized_claim: In the early hours of March 18, 1990, just after St. Patrick's Day, thieves robbed the Isabella Stewart Gardner Museum in Boston, and were inside for about 81 minutes.
allowed_wording: On March 18, 1990, the Gardner Museum was robbed.
The thieves were inside about 81 minutes.

claims.normalized_claim: Two men dressed as Boston police officers talked their way in by claiming to respond to a disturbance; a guard broke protocol and let them in, after which the thieves restrained the two guards in the basement (handcuffed, with their heads wrapped in duct tape), where they stayed until found the next morning.
allowed_wording: Two men disguised as police talked their way inside.
The guards were handcuffed and left in the basement.
claims.notes: Do not impute guilt to the guard who buzzed them in (see CLM-0014).

claims.normalized_claim: The thieves stole 13 works of art — cutting the two large Rembrandt canvases from their frames and removing others — and left at 2:45 a.m., making two trips to their car.
allowed_wording: They took 13 works in all.
Some canvases were cut right out of their frames.
prohibited_wording: They took everything in the museum.
claims.notes: Exactly 13; mix of paintings, drawings, and objects.

claims.normalized_claim: The 13 works included Rembrandt's 'Christ in the Storm on the Sea of Galilee' (1633, his only known seascape) and 'A Lady and Gentleman in Black', Vermeer's 'The Concert', Manet's 'Chez Tortoni', five works by Degas, Flinck's 'Landscape with an Obelisk', a small Rembrandt self-portrait etching, an ancient Chinese bronze Gu beaker, and a bronze eagle finial from a Napoleonic flagstaff.
counterevidence: Degas titles vary in spelling across sources; the set of five is solid.
allowed_wording: A Vermeer, two big Rembrandts, a Manet, five Degas, and a few objects.
Rembrandt's only seascape was among them.
claims.notes: Visuals: symbolic/empty-frame, not authentic reproductions of the real works.

claims.normalized_claim: Vermeer's 'The Concert' is described by the museum and the FBI as the most valuable unrecovered painting in the world.
allowed_wording: The Vermeer is called the most valuable missing painting in the world.
prohibited_wording: The Vermeer has a confirmed market price.
It is worth exactly X dollars.
claims.notes: Characterization by FBI/museum; not a market valuation.

claims.normalized_claim: The stolen works are estimated to be worth around $500 million, but that figure is an estimate, not a market price — the works are effectively unsellable, and the museum has said it is the only buyer for them.
allowed_wording: Estimated at around half a billion dollars.
Because no one can legally sell them, the price is really an estimate.
prohibited_wording: $500 million is the proven market value.
claims.notes: Always frame as estimate; the works are unsaleable.

claims.normalized_claim: Authorities characterize the theft as the largest of its kind — the FBI calls it the largest property crime in U.S. history, and the museum calls it the largest art heist in history.
counterevidence: 'Largest' is a characterization tied to the ~$500M estimate, not an audited ranking.
allowed_wording: The FBI calls it the largest property crime in U.S. history.
The museum calls it the largest art heist in history.
prohibited_wording: It is officially ranked #1 by an independent audit.

claims.normalized_claim: The case remains unsolved: none of the 13 works has ever been recovered, and the FBI continues to investigate.
allowed_wording: Decades later, it is still unsolved.
Not one of the 13 works has been found.
claims.notes: The hook and the ending hinge on this.

claims.normalized_claim: The empty frames still hang on the museum's walls — kept on display as a reminder of what was lost and a hope of return, in keeping with Isabella Stewart Gardner's wish that her collection be left arranged as she set it.
counterevidence: A 2009 court ruling relaxed the strict will terms to allow a 2012 addition, so 'nothing can ever change' is not absolute.
allowed_wording: The empty frames still hang where the paintings were.
They're left as a reminder, and a hope the art comes back.
prohibited_wording: The law makes it physically impossible to ever change the museum.
claims.notes: Iconic ending image; frame the 'why' carefully (will + symbolic choice).

claims.normalized_claim: The museum offers a $10 million reward for information leading to the recovery of all 13 works in good condition; the reward rose over time ($1 million, then $5 million in 1997, then $10 million in 2017, with the deadline later extended).
allowed_wording: There's a standing $10 million reward.
The reward climbed to $10 million.

claims.normalized_claim: The statute of limitations on the theft itself has expired, so the thieves can no longer be prosecuted for stealing the art; prosecutors have focused on recovery and signaled willingness to consider immunity for the return of the works, while still being able to charge possession, transport, or fraud tied to them.
counterevidence: Immunity was described as a 'strong possibility', not a blanket guarantee.
allowed_wording: The clock to charge the theft has run out.
Prosecutors care more about getting the art back than punishing the theft.
prohibited_wording: No one can ever be charged with anything.
claims.notes: Explains why recovery, not prosecution, drives the case now.

claims.normalized_claim: In 2013 the FBI said it believed, with high confidence, that it had identified the thieves — members of a criminal organization based in the Mid-Atlantic states and New England — but it declined to name them publicly; in 2015 the FBI said the two people who committed the heist are deceased. No one has ever been charged with the theft.
allowed_wording: The FBI says it believes it knows who did it — but has never named them, and says they are now dead.
No one has ever been charged with the theft.
claims.notes: R2 CORE. FBI never publicly named anyone. Do not name the thieves.

claims.normalized_claim: The FBI said its investigation traced the stolen art to Connecticut and the Philadelphia area, where some of it was offered for sale roughly a decade before 2013, but the FBI does not know its current location.
counterevidence: The sale was not consummated and the art was not recovered.
allowed_wording: The FBI says the art moved through Connecticut and the Philadelphia area.
Some was reportedly offered for sale years ago.
prohibited_wording: The art is currently in Philadelphia.
claims.notes: Attribute to FBI determination; location now unknown.

claims.normalized_claim: Over the decades many names have surfaced in connection with the heist — as suspects, persons of interest, or in theories — but none has ever been charged with the theft; some of those people are living and must not be portrayed as guilty, and the men sometimes described as the likely thieves were named by journalists, not by the FBI.
allowed_wording: Many names have come up over the years; none was ever charged with the theft.
The men often called the likely thieves were named by reporters, not the FBI.
prohibited_wording: The night guard was in on it.
claims.notes: R2 PROTECTIVE META-CLAIM. Use hedges; never assert guilt; flag living vs deceased; confirm any name before use.

claims.normalized_claim: One enduring puzzle: according to the museum's security chief, no motion sensor recorded anyone but a guard entering the Blue Room where Manet's 'Chez Tortoni' was taken, deepening the mystery of how that work was removed.
allowed_wording: Strangely, the sensors caught no thief entering the room where the Manet hung.
claims.notes: Use as an open puzzle, NOT as an accusation.

findings: R2 wording locks applied: never assert/imply guilt of any living person; FBI never publicly named the thieves; 'suspect/person of interest/investigated/never charged' hedges required; the believed thieves were named by journalists, not the FBI.
$500M is an ESTIMATE (works unsaleable, museum is 'the only buyer'); 'largest' is an attributed characterization.
Durable source content hashes PENDING (FBI/DOJ/museum pages bot-protected); non-blocking, capture before publish.
Before broadcast: confirm living/deceased status of any named person of interest; lock verbatim FBI/DOJ quotes on the live pages.
